examples.q: Water flows through a pipe and fills a 0.6 m³ tank in 20 seconds. What is the volumetric flow rate?
examples.solution:
Volumetric Flow Rate is the volume of fluid
passing a point per unit time.

Formula:
    Q = V / t

where  Q = volumetric flow rate  (m³/s)
       V = volume of fluid       (m³)
       t = time                  (s)

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    V = 0.6 m³
    t = 20 s

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    Q = 0.6 / 20
    Q = 0.03 m³/s

Answer:  Q = 0.03 m³/s

Note: Flow rate can also be expressed in
liters per second (L/s) or liters per minute (L/min).

examples.q: Water flows through a pipe with a cross-sectional area of 0.05 m² at a velocity of 4 m/s. What is the volumetric flow rate?
examples.solution:
Flow rate can also be calculated from the
cross-sectional area and the flow velocity:

    Q = A × v

where  Q = volumetric flow rate      (m³/s)
       A = cross-sectional area of pipe  (m²)
       v = velocity of fluid          (m/s)

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    A = 0.05 m²
    v = 4 m/s

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    Q = 0.05 × 4
    Q = 0.2 m³/s

Answer:  Q = 0.2 m³/s

Note: This formula comes from imagining a
cylinder of fluid moving through the pipe.

examples.q: Water flows from a wide pipe (A₁ = 0.1 m²) into a narrow pipe (A₂ = 0.02 m²). If the velocity in the wide pipe is 3 m/s, what is the velocity in the narrow pipe?
examples.solution:
By the Continuity Equation, flow rate is
conserved — fluid does not accumulate:

    Q₁ = Q₂
    A₁ × v₁ = A₂ × v₂

Rearranging to find v₂:
    v₂ = (A₁ × v₁) / A₂

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    A₁ = 0.1 m²,  v₁ = 3 m/s
    A₂ = 0.02 m²

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    v₂ = (0.1 × 3) / 0.02
    v₂ = 0.3 / 0.02
    v₂ = 15 m/s

Answer:  v₂ = 15 m/s

The fluid speeds up in the narrower pipe.
Smaller area → higher velocity.

questions.q: A pipe fills a 3 m³ tank in 60 seconds. What is the volumetric flow rate?
A pipe has a cross-sectional area of 0.08 m² and fluid flows at 2.5 m/s. What is the flow rate?
What is the unit of volumetric flow rate in the SI system?
questions.given:
Flow rate = Volume / Time.
Volume in m³, time in seconds.
questions.sol: The SI unit of volumetric flow rate is cubic meters per second (m³/s).

questions.q: A flow rate of 0.04 m³/s passes through a pipe of area 0.008 m². What is the fluid velocity?
questions.given:
Rearrange:  Q = A × v
→  v = Q / A

questions.q: How long does it take a pipe with flow rate 0.025 m³/s to fill a 0.5 m³ tank?
questions.given:
Rearrange:  Q = V / t
→  t = V / Q

questions.q: Fluid flows from pipe A (area = 0.06 m², v = 2 m/s) into pipe B (area = 0.03 m²). What is the velocity in pipe B?
questions.given:
Continuity Equation:  A₁ × v₁ = A₂ × v₂
→  v₂ = (A₁ × v₁) / A₂

questions.q: What happens to fluid velocity when it flows from a wide pipe into a narrow pipe?
questions.given:
Continuity Equation:  A₁v₁ = A₂v₂
If area decreases, what must happen to velocity?
questions.sol: By the Continuity Equation, if area decreases, velocity must increase to keep Q = Av constant.

questions.q: A flow rate of 0.1 m³/s flows through a pipe for 30 seconds. What volume of fluid passes through?
questions.given:
Rearrange:  Q = V / t
→  V = Q × t

questions.q: Pipe 1 (A = 0.2 m², v = 5 m/s) splits into two identical pipes. What is the flow rate in each branch?
questions.given:
Total flow rate Q = A × v.
Flow is shared equally between the two branches.
Q_each = Q_total / 2
questions.sol: Q_total = 0.2 × 5 = 1 m³/s. Each branch gets 1 / 2 = 0.5 m³/s.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly states the Continuity Equation for fluid flow?
questions.given:
This equation is based on the conservation of mass
for an incompressible fluid in a pipe.
opts: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂  (flow rate is constant throughout the pipe)
A₁v₁ = A₂v₂² (flow rate depends on velocity squared)
v₁ / A₁ = v₂ / A₂ (velocity divided by area is constant)
questions.sol: The Continuity Equation is A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ — the volumetric flow rate Q = Av is constant throughout an incompressible fluid flow.
